channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: International Sustainability Reporting – Divergence and Equivalence
item.description:
John Young is a Counsel, Ulysses Smith is an ESG Senior Advisor, and Alfie Scott is an Associate at Debevoise & Plimpton LLP. This post is based on their Debevoise memorandum.

Since the first application of the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (“CSRD”) to companies in 2024, two projects have been underway to produce detailed sustainability reporting standards: (i) the EU’s Sustainability Reporting Standards, with separate standards for EU companies (the “ESRS”) and groups with non-EU parents (the “N-ESRS”) reporting under CSRD and (ii) the International Sustainability Standards Board’s (“ISSB”) standards published by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation. As states around the world adopt sustainability reporting standards by reference to the ISSB standards, either by directly adopting those standards or by producing local standards derived from ISSB, world-wide groups will produce sustainability reports by reference to more than one set of standards.[1]

States and regulators now have the opportunity to address the divergence that has developed internationally. In this In Depth, we discuss the challenges raised by different sustainability standards around the world and the steps that states and authorities are taking to develop an equivalence or passporting system.

CSRD – Equivalence

CSRD contains a procedure for the European Commission (the “Commission”) to determine that the reporting standards of a non-EU state are “equivalent” to the ESRS. If the Commission makes an equivalence determination on the reporting standards of a non-EU state, a company in scope of CSRD may discharge its obligation under CSRD to produce a sustainability report by using those third-country standards in place of the ESRS or N-ESRS.[2]

Any such equivalence determination will require the Commission to pass a delegated act, using the following criteria to assess equivalence:

(a) that the sustainability reporting standards require companies to disclose information on environmental, social and governance factors; and

(B) that the sustainability reporting standards require companies to disclose information necessary to understand their impacts on sustainability matters and information necessary to understand how sustainability matters affect their development, performance and position.

Condition (b) refers to the fundamental concept underpinning CSRD sustainability reporting for EU companies under the ESRS, which is the double materiality assessment. Whilst an equivalence decision will necessarily be made by reference to the ISSB framework, the provisions in CSRD require the Commission to make individual decisions on a country-by-country basis.

CSRD – Position

We set out below the separate reporting obligations under CSRD, the relevant reporting standards and the position in relation to equivalence.

Reporting by public issuers (in scope from their 2024 financial years) Reporting by EU private companies (in scope from their 2027 financial years) Reporting by EU subsidiary companies with non-EU ultimate parent companies (in scope from their 2028 financial years) Parent company reportingCompanies which are in scope of CSRD by having transferable securities admitted to trading on an EU-regulated market may not discharge their reporting obligation by reliance on a consolidated parent report.Subject to conditions, private EU companies in scope of CSRD may discharge their reporting obligation by reliance on a consolidated parent report, including a report published by a non-EU parent company.

Until 2030, private EU companies in scope of CSRD which share the same non-EU parent can also delegate preparation of the CSRD report to the largest EU company in scope.

 EU subsidiary companies or branches which meet specific size thresholds are required to publish a report at the group level of their ultimate non-EU parent company if the parent company generates a certain level of turnover in the European Union. Relevant reporting standards EU ESRS, with topics selected on the basis of double materiality (financial and impact). These companies in scope of 2024 should report under the “original” ESRS, with the Commission intending to introduce simplified ESRS shortly. EU ESRS, with topics selected on the basis of double materiality (financial and impact). N-ESRS, with topics selected on the basis of impact materiality only. EquivalenceNot applicable.Where an EU company is a subsidiary of a non-EU parent company, it may discharge its reporting obligations by using the third country’s reporting standards if considered equivalent to the ESRS.The EU company may discharge its reporting obligations by using the N-ESRS, ESRS or a third country’s reporting standards if considered equivalent to the ESRS.[3]

Differences Between ESRS and ISSB

There are two key differences between the ESRS and ISSB standards:

  • For the ESRS, double materiality is the basis for sustainability reporting—meaning that EU companies in scope of CSRD should determine material information to be reported on the basis of impact materiality, when a topic pertains to the company’s material actual or potential positive or negative impacts on people or the environment, or financial materiality, when a topic generates risks or opportunities that affect the company’s financial position, financial performance, cash flows, access to finance or cost of capital. Again, EU subsidiaries of non-EU groups which are in scope of N-ESRS are only required to report on impact, rather than financial, materiality.
  • Whilst ISSB, to date, comprises a standard for general reporting requirements and a standard for climate-related disclosure,[4]the ESRS comprise general, climate and nine additional reporting standards dedicated to additional environmental and social topics. Under ISSB, companies report on topics other than climate under a general requirement to identify and disclose information on other sustainability topics, with entities required to consider disclosure topics included in the Sustainability Account Standards Board (SASB) Standards, which contain industry-by-industry lists of relevant sustainability topics and metrics, and may, in reporting on relevant topics, use the ESRS topic standards for reporting beyond climate reporting.

As above, the Commission has neither produced an equivalence decision nor indicated that it is working on any such decision. However, under the draft N-ESRS, a reporting company will be allowed to cross-refer to information it publishes in a separate sustainability report which it prepares under national law of a third country, for example, under a national law requirement report under ISSB, to meet a disclosure requirement under the N-ESRS. In addition, to assist companies’ sustainability reporting, EFRAG, the body that advises the Commission on sustainability reporting, and the IFRS Foundation published in May 2024 “inter-operability” guidance.[5] This shows a high degree of alignment between the climate-related disclosures in both sets of standards and confirms that the concept of “financial materiality” in the ESRS is aligned with the concept of “materiality” in the ISSB’s standards, as well as common defined terms in the two sets of standards. The inter-operability guidance leaves open the following points:

  • that the ESRS double-materiality assessment requires companies to consider impact, as well as financial, materiality when selecting material information to report;
  • that the ESRS includes topic specific standards, with detailed reporting requirements in each standard.

Review of ESRS

As part of the Commission’s Omnibus review, the ESRS are undergoing considerable simplification, with the Commission publishing draft revised ESRS for consultation in May 2026. During the process, various groups lobbied for the ESRS better to align to the ISSB standards, with at least one proposal that the ESRS should allow companies to report information under financial materiality and impact materiality in separate sections, thereby allowing companies to publish an ISSB-compliant report, with add-on material to satisfy ESRS impact materiality. In the event, the draft revised ESRS contained no such flexibility, reflecting an unwillingness on the part of the Commission to introduce any type of hierarchy between financial and impact factors, and arguably undermine the European Union’s double-materiality principle.

The draft N-ESRS state that if a reporting company prepares sustainability information covering risks and opportunities under a local reporting framework, it may refer to that information in its N-ESRS report, provided it presents the information in a neutral way to give a balanced presentation of positive and negative contextual information.

Conclusion

In the Commission’s review of the ESRS and N-ESRS, it did not significantly advance equivalence of these with ISSB standards, nor restructure the ESRS to accommodate better companies that report under both standards. Given any equivalence decision is conditional on the non-EU sustainability reporting standards embodying the double-materiality principle, it is unclear whether the European Union will ever consider ISSB-derived standards as fully equivalent.

Both the ISSB and the European Union have emphasised the close connection between financial and impact materiality. In its guidance, the ISSB states that “Describing ISSB Standards as ‘outside-in’ is an incomplete, and therefore incorrect, description. The effect of an entity’s activities on resources and relationships—including on people and the environment—might give rise to sustainability-related risks and opportunities that could reasonably be expected to affect the entity’s prospects and thus are relevant when applying ISSB standards.” The European Union has also been clear that many matters may be material from both an impact and a financial perspective—in other words, that financially material topics often arise either from impacts that a company makes on society or the environment, such as pollution, or from a company’s “dependencies” on natural or human resources, such as water use or elements of its supply chain which are prone to environmental risk. The proposed N-ESRS standards state that reporting on impacts only under the standards does not exclude non-EU groups from reporting on impacts which do or may result in sustainability-related risks and opportunities. It is also worth noting that, under ISSB, companies report on financial risks and opportunities linked to sustainability that can be expected to affect a company’s prospects—without specifying a minimum financial impact or time horizon, which the company will determine.

The interplay between impact and financial materiality reflects the European Union’s unwillingness to separate each materiality lens into two separate parts of the report. In practice, one distinction between impact and financial materiality may be that certain impacts that a company identifies will not become financially material for many years—although it is unclear whether companies will wish to distinguish reporting on material impacts on this basis.

1 We omit consideration of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission’s efforts to develop climate-related reporting requirements, which the SEC has recently moved to rescind, as well as U.S. state-level initiatives to mandate GHG emissions and climate risk reporting, such as in California.(go back)

2 At the time of writing, the ESRS are in near-final form and will be adopted shortly as a Delegated Act before entering into force following approval from the EU institutions. EFRAG has published the N-ESRS, which are subject to the Commission’s input.(go back)

3 Note that CSRD contemplates the Commission determining that a third country’s reporting standards are equivalent to the full EU ESRS, as opposed to the non-EU ESRS. Hence, a company that has prepared a sustainability report in accordance with the Global Sustainability Standards Board’s Global Reporting Initiative, which covers impact reporting, cannot discharge its obligations under N-ESRS.(go back)

4 The ISSB has indicated that it is working to incorporate nature-related disclosures into IFRS S1 and S2 rather than releasing new standards.(go back)

5 This guidance relates to the “pre-Omnibus” ESRS and as a result may change.(go back)

item.title: Flood deaths in West African megacities raise fraught issue of slum evictions
item.description: This has further heightened flood risks across West African cities. Kiswendsida Guigma, technical advisor at the Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Centre ...

item.title: FCA product-level simplification 'risks lowering market standards', says PRI
item.description:
Create an account to continue reading

Gain instant access to our expert editorial analysis and in-depth insight.

Register for free

Industry body urges regulator to retain core mandatory set of metrics to keep level playing field and avoid shifting burden to clients.

Copyright PEI Media

Not for publication, email or dissemination
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Turning Flood Alerts into Community Action in Djibouti - World Meteorological Organization WMO
item.description:
Turning Flood Alerts into Community Action in Djibouti

In Djibouti, floods don’t arrive slowly. Although sporadic, when heavy rain falls and water levels rise, communities must act fast. But crucial early warnings don’t reach everyone equally. Access to information, mobility and caregiving roles affect how effectively people can respond to flood warnings issued by local authorities. Ensuring that early warnings reach everyone is critical for their effectiveness.

In the flood-prone neighbourhood of Vietnam in Djibouti City, a community member receives a notice that water levels nearby are rising fast. She quickly warns her neighbours, using a megaphone to spread the word. She goes from door to door to make sure that everybody receives the alert, especially those without access to digital communication. But this community relay is only one element of a wider, integrated approach that helps reduce the destructive impact of floods on local communities.

Floods are among Djibouti’s most damaging hazards, destroying livelihoods, disrupting development and claiming lives. In the densely populated neighbourhoods of the capital region, closely packed housing and a lack of drainage infrastructure can cause floodwater to accumulate rapidly during intense rainfall, leaving residents with little time to react. Effective early warnings are therefore critical. Yet a warning only saves lives if it reaches people and enables them to act quickly.

Putting inclusion into practice

The steps community members take in response to floods are set out in the Community Flood Action Plan, recently developed by the Djibouti Red Crescent Society (DRCS) under the Climate Risk and Early Warning Systems (CREWS) Djibouti Project, led by the World Meteorological Organization (WMO). Putting the plan into practice, however, requires preparation.

In May 2026, DRCS organized a community-based drill simulating a flood scenario and testing the established inclusive evacuation procedures. The exercise mobilized community volunteers and placed particular emphasis on vulnerable groups, such as the elderly, caregivers, and those with limited mobility, who need special assistance to evacuate the area.

“Through this exercise, we want to see the self-organization of the community and also their preparedness capacity”, explains Hawa Ibrahim of DRCS, “because we know that a community that has its capacity strengthened is a community that is better prepared for all the disaster risk situations”.

Building a system of inclusion

In the run-up to the drill, a two-day workshop on “Inclusion for Better Alerts” brought together national meteorological and disaster management institutions, local governments, NGOs and community representatives to explore how gender and social inclusion can be better integrated throughout End-to-End Early Warning Systems for Floods (E2E-EWS-FF).

Participants discussed how to make early warning systems more inclusive, from planning and programming to monitoring and budgeting. The workshop outcomes delivered a strong collective message: inclusive early warning systems cannot remain a principle on paper. Participants called for sustained investment, stronger institutional coordination, accessible alerts for all and concrete implementation, reaffirming that without inclusion, early warnings cannot fully protect the communities most at risk.

Together, the workshop and the simulation drill marked an important step for Djibouti, representing the country’s first coordinated efforts to integrate gender and social inclusion into early warning systems.

These efforts reflect the broader ambition of the CREWS Djibouti project: to build an early warning ecosystem that is not only technically robust but also people-centred, inclusive and effective. By strengthening coordination between institutions, improving forecasting and communication capacities, and empowering communities to act before disasters strike, CREWS Djibouti is helping transform early warnings into early action.

Beyond systems and structures, the real test of early warning lies in its ability to reach everyone in time and allow them to take action. When inclusion becomes the foundation, early warning systems stop being abstract mechanisms and become catalysts for community action, ensuring no one is left behind.

  • WMO Member:
  • Djibouti
  • Region:
  • Region I: Africa
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Connecting Monsoon Science and Services - World Meteorological Organization WMO
item.description:
Connecting Monsoon Science and Services

In July 2022, Pakistan experienced one of the most devastating monsoon seasons in its history. A single weather station in Sindh province recorded more than 1 600 millimetres of rain in a single day. By the end of the season, the country had received 175% of its normal annual rainfall, severely disrupting the lives of around 12 million people.

Monsoons shape the lives of more than two-thirds of the world's population across Asia, Africa, Australia and the Americas, from densely populated cities to Small Island Developing States (SIDS). Monsoon extremes rarely arrive alone. Floods, droughts, heatwaves and landslides can strike in sequence or simultaneously, creating cascading impacts that are among the greatest challenges for operational meteorology today.

Against this backdrop, scientists, forecasters and policymakers gathered in Shanghai in May for the Open Monsoon Conference 2026 (OMC2026), co-organized by the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the China Meteorological Administration (CMA) and locally hosted by the Chinese Academy of Meteorological Sciences (CAMS) and the Shanghai Meteorological Service. The conference examined how advances in monsoon science, observations and forecasting can better support the services that help communities prepare for increasingly complex weather and climate risks.

A science still evolving

Monsoon science has advanced considerably over recent decades. Researchers now have a much better understanding of the large-scale climate drivers that influence monsoon variability. such as the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), the Indian Ocean Dipole and the Madden-Julian Oscillation. Forecasting systems have also improved, with seasonal outlooks increasingly supporting agriculture, water management and disaster preparedness.

These advances are already making a difference. Since 2007, Myanmar has held a national monsoon forum twice a year, bringing together forecasters, agricultural agencies and disaster management authorities to translate seasonal outlooks into practical guidance. In India, seasonal forecasts with lead times of three to six months are now informing agricultural planning and water resource management, where even a 10% deviation in rainfall can put significant stress on food and water systems. In Afghanistan, the national meteorological service issues flash flood and landslide warnings at least six hours in advance, giving communities in mountainous areas time to reach safety.

Even so, discussions at OMC2026 highlighted that important challenges remain. Maintaining scientific rigour and extending forecast lead times remain a priority. Artificial intelligence (AI) is opening new possibilities, but current AI-based models tend to underpredict the intensity of extreme events. The emerging consensus is that AI should complement physical understanding, not replace it.

The gaps that remain

On the scientific side, models systematically overestimate the strength of the ENSO-monsoon relationship. Monsoon onset and withdrawal also remain difficult to predict with useful lead times, while extending reliable forecast skill to subseasonal-to-seasonal timescales is a particular priority. The potential impact of climate tipping elements on monsoon regions was also identified as an area requiring further research.

When it comes to observations, regional coordinated aircraft missions are improving typhoon observations, while satellite data is increasingly filling gaps where surface networks cannot reach. But uncertainties in ocean surface fluxes remain large, and in many regions surface networks are too sparse to capture the processes that drive extreme events. What is needed is not only additional observations, but better targeted observations shared more openly across borders.

Operational services face additional constraints. Global models developed primarily for mid-latitude conditions do not always perform well in the tropics. Some representatives noted that as many as half of their automatic weather stations are not functioning at any given time. For SIDS, the stakes are especially high. As Abdulla Wahid, Permanent Representative of the Maldives with WMO put it, "We are a little far away from the main continent, and it seems that we are left on our own to manage our situation. And it is not so easy".

From research to resilience

None of these gaps can be closed by any one country working alone. Strengthening the full value chain, from coordinated observations and improved forecasting to warnings that reach the communities most at risk, requires sustained international collaboration. That collaboration must also be more inclusive.

Representatives from Africa and Indian Ocean island states noted that southern hemisphere monsoon systems received far less attention at the conference than the boreal summer monsoon systems of Asia. A truly global monsoon science cannot afford these blind spots. The United Nations' Early Warnings for All initiative provides a framework for a more connected and equitable effort. But frameworks only work if the institutional connections they require are built and maintained.

OMC2026 brought together 149 participants in Shanghai and up to 150 online attendees from 41 countries, with around two-thirds of them early career professionals. The conference will produce scientific outcomes and recommendations, intended to support continued discussion and collaboration. The questions raised in Shanghai will not be answered quickly. But addressing them will be essential to strengthening resilience in monsoon-affected regions.

  • Region:
  • Region II: Asia
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: 'Critical dependency' on single hospital is major disaster risk - MaltaToday
item.description: World Bank warns Malta still lacks enforceable seismic, fire safety building codes · Fire and earthquake risks · Climate change increasing risks.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: AI-altered images on birdwatching forums putting research at risk - The Guardian
item.description:
For many birdwatchers, recording a species outside its normal range is the holy grail. In the UK, the discoveries often make national headlines. The western reef heron, for example, usually found in Africa and southern Europe, was spotted in a seaside town in north Wales in June, and widely celebrated on birding forums.

But a new scourge is threatening to disrupt the fun: AI slop.

Scientists are appealing to birders to limit their use of AI when editing images over fears that it could undermine the credibility of popular citizen science platforms such as iNaturalist and Macaulay Library, which are routinely used in scientific research to monitor species’ habitat range.

The rise of generative AI platforms such as ChatGPT and Google Gemini has led to a sharp increase in fake and enhanced images of rare and celebrated birds across wildlife photography forums. Users can create high-quality fake images in seconds – or enhance a photograph by asking the AI to remove a branch or leaf that might be obscuring part of the subject, which can inadvertently introduce significant changes.

In a recent commentary in the science journal Nature, researchers warned that hundreds of fake images have already been discovered on popular databases for recording species. They said that the true scale of the issue is unknown – as many could go undetected – and could contaminate records collected by the public.

“My experience of looking at Facebook these days is that a huge volume of wildlife photos now are simply AI-generated imagery,” said Dr Alexander Lees, an ecologist at Manchester Metropolitan University who authored the journal article. “The idea that we could maybe use those photos to help us understand where species are in space and time is very difficult.”

Outright hoaxes remain rare and are often easy to spot, said Lees – nobody is falling for a toucan sighting in Siberia – but he says birders often use AI to edit and improve an image, and the algorithm can then introduce parts from different bird species to create the new image.

Lees points to the example of a false sighting of a red-winged blackbird in central Brazil, which is usually found in North America and had never been seen in this area of Brazil until the reported sighting. The bird was, in fact, an epaulet oriole – a common new world bird species – but the photographer had asked an AI platform to make the picture “look better”, which is when parts of the red-winged blackbird were added, resulting in the false sighting.

“Wildlife photographers can be quite obsessed with getting a beautiful photo, but there’s a risk that the image might actually cause problems down the line when AI has been used to edit it,” said Lees.

Citizen science organisations are still working to understand the scale of the issue. On iNaturalist, a social network where nature enthusiasts can record sightings of plants and animals, just 1,400 of the more than 610m images on the platform have been flagged for AI use. From monitoring how plants and animals move in response to climate breakdown to recording new behaviour in species, dozens of discoveries have been made using citizen science.

Tony Iwane, iNaturalist’s director of community support, who was a co-author on the Nature paper, said most of these cases were unlikely to be malicious, but appealed to users to be vigilant.

“On platforms like ours, regular people are posting information that a scientist could probably never get at scale. It is also almost like a sensor of what is happening on Earth in real time: are plants flowering early? Are species moving north as the climate warms? The more we know about where species are, the better informed we can be as conservationists. But the information needs to be accurate,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: AI gives utilities a chance to climate-proof the grid - Sustainable Views
item.description: The AI boom is forcing utilities to spend billions of dollars expanding and modernising electric grids around the world. Investors say the influx of investment is also giving utility companies the opportunity to build climate resilience into infrastructure that was overdue for renewal.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Eight lifesaving ways to improve wildfire risk management - The Conversation
item.description:
As the flames advanced towards the village of Bédar, in the Almería province of Andalusia, the mayor gave a desperate order to ring the church bells to warn residents. It was not enough. The July 9 Los Gallardos fire, which claimed 13 lives and burnt 7,000 hectares, was Spain’s third deadliest on record.

Most of the victims were retired foreigners living in isolated homes, who died trapped in their vehicles or fleeing on foot along dead-end paths in a landscape dominated by scrubland and rugged terrain, most of which is difficult to access.

High temperatures and extremely dry weather turn scrub, grasslands and bushes into highly flammable fuel. This, combined with complex terrain that makes fire spread faster and unpredictably, poses an enormous challenge for firefighting efforts.

Andalusian legislation requires local emergency plans for forest fires in high-risk areas, and self-protection plans for detached buildings and housing estates. However, there is a widespread problem with compliance and implementation of these measures.

Fires are changing

The disaster in Los Gallardos shows that this problem will only get worse, unless we take decisive and effective action that goes beyond the current dominant paradigm of simply extinguishing and resisting fire.

Where, when and how fires occur is changing. Highly destructive fires are on the rise, housing is expanding into the urban-forest interface. This exposes more homes to fire, and the the risk is increasing most quickly in scrubland and grassland areas, which are not typically associated with fire risk.

This is a major problem for communities that do not know how to respond to a risk they have not often been exposed to. We therefore need to urgently rethink how fire risk is managed.

Read more: Spain wildfires: with 13 dead, we need to start measuring fire damage in lives – not just hectares

Improving fire resilience

It is unsustainable to respond only after a disaster has happened. What we need are strategies based on preparedness, mitigation and adaptation. The following eight points are particularly important in achieving this.

1. Manage social vulnerability

Wildfires exacerbate the existing social vulnerabilities that affect people’s ability to anticipate, cope with, withstand and recover from them. In high-risk areas the population may consist of elderly people, people living alone in isolated homes, with financial constraints, or who do not know the local area well.

Fires also pose a risk to local economies. Businesses – including those that provide public services and infrastructure – can be vulnerable for a range of reasons, including flammable facilities, their location, or disruptions to supply chains, essential services and staff mobility.

Targeted planning can identify and address all of these vulnerabilities.

2. Adopt nature-based solutions

Nature-based solutions have not been fully integrated into fire risk management, even though they are widely recognised as essential parts of climate change adaptation and nature restoration.

Nature-based solutions include controlled grazing, the creation of less flammable habitats, green firebreaks, and the restoration of water networks to increase ambient humidity and create areas that slow the spread of fire. These measures also bring many additional benefits for the local population and economy.

3. Create (and update) emergency, self-protection and training plans

Local authorities must draw up emergency plans that the public can access. Isolated dwellings and housing estates must have their own coordinated plans. The plans should include fire hydrants and water supply points, accessible warning systems, access and evacuation routes, and protocols for vulnerable people.

But plans alone are not enough without training. There is also a need for ongoing programmes, tailored to each community, that train people to safely maintain their homes and surroundings, familiarise them with meeting points and shelter areas, and teach them to distinguish between lockdown and evacuation.

Drills are essential. Evacuation is not the same as fleeing, and emergency response must be planned and practised.

4. Promote collective action

Risk reduction is most effective when it is community-based, as this increases overall collective resilience. This means public authorities, community organisations, and the public and private sector must all work alongside one another.

The public should also be actively involved in the design of emergency plans. This builds legitimacy, improves the capacity to respond to fires, and fosters trust and a sense of shared responsibility for preventing fires.

5. Promote integrated and coordinated governance

Fire risk management cannot be the sole responsibility of forestry or firefighting services – it needs an integrated system. All agencies that act in the area must take fire risk into account when making decisions.

In practice, this means avoiding new construction and establishing specific building regulations in high-risk areas, creating buffer zones between different land uses, and promoting uses that help to maintain fire-resilient landscapes.

6. Incentivise risk management

Economic tools and behavioural insights can be leveraged to make self-protection measures and land management the easiest and most rewarding options. This includes nudges: interventions that are designed to encourage but not force people to make certain decisions.

One example of this is payments for environmental services, which financially incentivise land management practices that reduce wildfire risk.

The role of insurance is also evolving in the face of natural and climate-related risks. Instruments are being designed based on a proactive model that incentivises individual and collective risk-reduction behaviour.

7. Improve monitoring and advance warning systems

Up-to-date information on a fire’s development is essential. Real-time monitoring systems, sensors, satellite imagery, predictive models of fire behaviour and alert applications providing detailed information can all improve response capabilities and enable safer evacuations. Integrating these tools into local management plans is a necessary investment.

8. Encourage public and private investment in long-term preparedness

Investments in prevention and preparedness provide significant returns. Growing requirements – both regulatory and voluntary – for the private sector to disclose information on nature-related risks and dependencies are generating new demand for investment products (carbon and biodiversity credits, habitat banks, environmental impact bonds, and so on).

This growing private investment must now be channelled towards measures that strengthen both people’s and the landscape’s wildfire resilience. This is to supplement, not replace public investment.

The Los Gallardos blaze serves as a reminder that we need to fundamentally change the way we understand and manage fire risk. Investment in preparedness and coordinated planning between public authorities, the private sector and the general public could be the key to ensuring that, should the alarm bells ring again, the population is ready to respond.

A weekly e-mail in English featuring expertise from scholars and researchers. It provides an introduction to the diversity of research coming out of the continent and considers some of the key issues facing European countries. Get the newsletter!
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Early Wildfire Detection System Launch in Spain - Allianz.com
item.description:
Last updated: March 31, 2026

Allianz launches in Spain an early wildfire detection system using artificial intelligence and satellite technology

At a glance:

  • The initiative aims to strengthen prevention and response capacity against one of the country’s main climate risks
  • The technology reduces average detection times by 35 minutes compared to reference systems such as NASA’s, and detects up to 30% more fires
  • Allianz will share the information with public authorities and proactively inform clients if their assets are close to fire hotspots

During the summer, the project will be developed at a national level, monitoring more than 50 million hectares through artificial intelligence, advanced satellite observation, and predictive models capable of detecting heat sources and anticipating their evolution, in an initial implementation phase to validate the scope and effectiveness of these new capabilities.

This effort comes at a critical moment: in 2025, Spain recorded the worst fire year of the past three decades, with 354,793 hectares devastated and nearly 500,000 hectares burned since 2023.

At the same time, the economic impact continues to grow. In 2025, fires caused losses approaching 5 billion euros, while insurance sector payouts reached 770 million, according to UNESPA.

This is compounded by the climate context. A recent Allianz Trade study places Spain among the European economies most exposed to extreme heat, with potential losses of up to 104 billion euros by 2030. The sustained rise in temperatures and heatwaves is generating drier conditions that favor the spread of fires, while putting additional pressure on infrastructure, energy, and public finances, reducing response capacity.

“Wildfires have become one of the main climate risks for Spain. As an insurer, our commitment is no longer limited to supporting people when an incident occurs but extends to anticipating risk and actively helping to prevent it. Projects like this reflect that ambition: thanks to artificial intelligence and satellite observation, we can detect fires earlier and gain crucial time to protect people, the land, and economic activity,” explains Veit Stutz, CEO of Allianz Seguros.

From incident response to risk prevention

The insurance sector is evolving from a model focused on compensation after an incident to one based on anticipation and prevention. Within this framework, Allianz is exploring new technological capabilities to better protect individuals, businesses, and infrastructure.

The solution developed together with Satellites on Fire combines data from multiple satellite constellations—both geostationary and polar—increasing observation frequency and detection accuracy.

“Spain faces the same challenge that led us to create Satellites on Fire in Argentina. Fires don’t wait and conventional systems arrive late. Working with Allianz allows us to bring our learnings from Latin America to Europe, where we have already demonstrated that earlier detection saves forests, communities, and lives,” states Franco Rodríguez Viau, CEO and co-founder of Satellites on Fire.

According to the project, the technology can reduce detection times by about 35 minutes compared to systems like NASA’s, identify up to 30% more fires, and reduce false negatives by 40% in early stages.

More protected clients through technology

A distinctive element is its direct application to clients. Allianz will be able to proactively inform policyholders when insured assets are near fire sources with potential for spread, facilitating decision-making and improving response.

“We want our clients to be better prepared for future risks. Technology allows us to move towards smarter, more preventive, and more useful protection,” says Stutz.

The company has also explored ways to collaborate with public authorities to share information and strengthen prevention at the territorial level, in a context where cooperation among the private sector, technology, and institutions is key.

This initiative is part of Allianz’s innovation strategy, focused on collaborating with startups and accelerating solutions with real impact, combining technological innovation, prevention, and protection against major climate risks.

About Allianz Seguros

Allianz Seguros is the main subsidiary of the Allianz Group in Spain and one of the leading companies in the Spanish insurance sector. To deliver the best results for its clients, the company is committed to physical proximity (through its branches and regional offices with more than 2,300 employees and its network of over 13,000 agents) and technological advancement (through tools such as its app for smartphones and tablets, its eClient area on the corporate website, and more than 500,000 SMS messages sent annually to its clients).

It offers one of the most comprehensive and innovative product ranges on the market and is based on the concept of total security. For this reason, the products and services provided by the company cover personal and family needs as well as business needs, offering life, auto, home, accident, and health insurance, as well as multi-risk insurance for companies and businesses, up to the most complex personalized insurance solutions.

About Satellites on Fire

Further information

More news

About Allianz

The Allianz Group is one of the world’s leading insurers and asset managers, active in almost 70 countries and serving around 97 million private and corporate customers*. Our customers benefit from a broad range of personal and corporate insurance services, including property, life and health insurance, as well as assistance services, credit and global business insurance. Recognized for the seventh consecutive year as the number one global insurance brand in Interbrand’s Best Global Brands 2025 ranking, Allianz’s success is built on technology-enabled customer centricity – providing peace of mind, protection, and prevention for our customers and strengthening the resilience of individuals, communities, and societies. We are one of the world’s largest investors, managing around 770 billion euros on behalf of our insurance customers. Furthermore, our asset managers PIMCO and Allianz Global Investors manage about 2.0 trillion euros of third-party assets. Thanks to our systematic integration of environmental and social criteria in our business processes and investment decisions, Allianz received an MSCI ESG Rating of AAA (as of March 2026). In 2025, our 156,000 dedicated employees achieved a total business volume of 186.9 billion euros and an operating profit of 17.4 billion euros for our shareholders.

* As of December 31, 2025. Customer count reflects Allianz customers in consolidated entities that are part of the customer reporting scope only
